content.text: What It Is

WordPiece and SentencePiece are two subword tokenization algorithms that supersede simple Byte-Pair Encoding in several of the most influential language models. WordPiece was developed at Google and published alongside the BERT model in 2018; SentencePiece is a language-agnostic tokenization library, also from Google, that ships with LLaMA, T5, mT5, XLNet, and Albert. Both produce subword vocabularies — splitting rare words into meaningful fragments while keeping frequent words as atomic tokens — but they differ fundamentally in how they select merges, how they handle raw text, and how they achieve language independence.

WordPiece extends BPE by changing the merge criterion: instead of selecting the most frequent adjacent pair, it selects the pair whose merge maximizes the likelihood of the training corpus under the current vocabulary model. This likelihood-based objective forces WordPiece to build a vocabulary of pieces that are jointly most useful for compressing the corpus, rather than greedily merging whatever is locally most frequent. In practice WordPiece also prefixes word-internal pieces with '##' to distinguish them from word-initial pieces — a convention that BERT and its successors expose directly in the tokenizer output.

SentencePiece takes a different architectural position: it treats raw input bytes as the training signal with no whitespace-based pre-tokenization step. Every space, newline, and punctuation character is just another byte. This design makes SentencePiece intrinsically language-agnostic — Japanese, Thai, and Arabic text, which have no word-delimiting spaces, tokenize just as naturally as English. SentencePiece supports two underlying algorithms: BPE and Unigram Language Model. The Unigram variant is unique: rather than building a vocabulary bottom-up by merging, it starts with a very large candidate set and prunes tokens whose removal causes the smallest drop in corpus log-likelihood, converging to a smaller target vocabulary.
Why It Matters

The tokenizer is the first and last thing that touches text in a language model pipeline: at the input it converts raw strings to integer IDs, at the output it converts IDs back to human-readable text. Every design decision in the tokenizer — vocabulary size, subword splitting criterion, whitespace handling, Unicode normalization — propagates forward into the model's embedding table, training dynamics, and inference behavior. Using the wrong tokenizer with a given model checkpoint produces token ID mismatches that cause completely incoherent model output, with no runtime error to flag the problem.

WordPiece's '##' prefix convention has semantic implications for BERT-style models: the embedding for '##ing' is trained separately from the embedding for 'ing' at the start of a word, and the model learns that '##' pieces depend on their left neighbor in a way that stand-alone pieces do not. Masking strategies in BERT's MLM objective also operate at the WordPiece level — whole-word masking requires reconstructing the '##' suffix pieces that belong to the same word as the masked token. Understanding this is essential when adapting BERT for domain-specific fine-tuning or extending its vocabulary.

SentencePiece's whitespace-agnostic design matters most in production systems that handle diverse languages. A single SentencePiece model trained on a multilingual corpus achieves balanced token fertility across typologically different languages: agglutinative (Turkish, Finnish), logographic (Chinese, Japanese), and morphologically sparse (English). This balance is critical because imbalanced fertility means that prompts in one language consume 3 to 5 times more context tokens than equivalent prompts in another, with downstream effects on cost, latency, and effective context window.
Benefits
Likelihood-optimal vocabulary (WordPiece): pieces are selected to maximize corpus compression by PMI rather than raw pair frequency, producing a more globally useful vocabulary.

Language-agnostic tokenization (SentencePiece): works on raw bytes without language-specific pre-tokenization rules, making it straightforward to apply to new languages and scripts without code changes.

Unigram LM pruning (SentencePiece Unigram): starts from a large candidate set and prunes, ensuring every token in the final vocabulary is genuinely necessary rather than historically accumulated from greedy merges.

Reversible whitespace encoding (SentencePiece): the '▁' prefix on word-initial pieces allows perfect reconstruction of the original string including spaces, eliminating lossy detokenization artifacts.
Open vocabulary: both algorithms handle any Unicode input without an unknown-token fallback by decomposing OOV text to known character or byte-level pieces.

Subword regularization (SentencePiece Unigram): the probabilistic segmentation model supports data augmentation by sampling alternative tokenizations of the same word during training, improving model robustness to tokenization variance.
Trade-offs
WordPiece training cost: the likelihood-maximization criterion requires evaluating corpus likelihood at each merge step, making WordPiece training significantly slower than BPE on large corpora.

SentencePiece Unigram ambiguity: Unigram LM tokenization is not deterministic by default — multiple valid segmentations may have similar probability, and subword regularization samples from this distribution during training. This must be disabled at inference time via nbest_size=0.
WordPiece prefix coupling: models trained with WordPiece are coupled to the '##' prefix scheme. Altering the prefix convention requires retraining the model from scratch.
SentencePiece raw bytes can propagate encoding inconsistencies (NFC vs. NFKC normalization) unless Unicode normalization is explicitly configured at training time.
Vocabulary portability: a tokenizer trained on one corpus is not directly transferable to a new model trained on a different corpus — the embedding table dimensions must match exactly.
How It Works
WordPiece: Likelihood-Based Merging

Like BPE, WordPiece starts from a character-level vocabulary with each word split into individual characters. At each merge step, rather than choosing the most frequent adjacent pair, it chooses the pair (A, B) that maximizes the increase in corpus log-likelihood when A and B are merged into AB. The log-likelihood gain for a candidate merge is approximately proportional to a pointwise mutual information (PMI) score:

This PMI criterion favors pairs that co-occur more than would be expected if A and B were independent pieces. A pair with count(AB) = 1000 but count(A) = 10,000 and count(B) = 10,000 scores much lower than a pair with count(AB) = 100, count(A) = 105, count(B) = 105, because the latter pair almost exclusively occurs together. The PMI criterion produces a vocabulary where merged tokens represent genuine linguistic units rather than artifacts of raw frequency.

WordPiece also applies a two-prefix convention: pieces that can start a word carry no prefix, while pieces that can only appear inside a word are prefixed with '##'. When encoding the word 'playing', if 'play' and '##ing' are both in the vocabulary, the result is ['play', '##ing']. The '##' prefix is stored as part of the token string and reflected in the integer ID. During decoding, tokens with '##' are concatenated directly to the previous token without inserting a space.
SentencePiece: Raw-Text Unigram LM

SentencePiece trains directly on raw Unicode text, treating whitespace as just another character. To mark word boundaries after tokenization, it uses the '▁' (U+2581, lower one-eighth block) prefix on pieces that follow a space. This is the inverse of BPE's end-of-word marker: instead of flagging word endings, SentencePiece flags word beginnings, making it trivial to reconstruct the original spaced text by replacing '▁' with a space.

The Unigram LM algorithm starts with a seed vocabulary of all possible substrings up to a fixed length (typically 16 characters), which can be millions of candidates. It then iteratively prunes: at each step it removes the fraction of tokens whose deletion causes the smallest total decrease in training corpus log-likelihood. The probability assigned to each token piece is:

During encoding, the Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable segmentation of each input string under this unigram language model. Unlike BPE and WordPiece, there is a well-defined probability for every possible segmentation, which enables subword regularization: during training, the model samples from the top-k most probable segmentations rather than always using the best, teaching the downstream model to be robust to alternative tokenizations of the same word.
Architecture
The diagram below contrasts the WordPiece and SentencePiece pipelines side by side, from raw text input to the final token ID sequence emitted into the model's embedding lookup.

attrs.alt: WordPiece and SentencePiece tokenization pipelines compared: WordPiece requires pre-tokenization and uses ## prefix; SentencePiece uses raw bytes and uses the lower-block prefix on word-initial pieces

content.text: The left column shows WordPiece's two-phase pipeline: a whitespace and punctuation split followed by subword lookup using the '##' prefix convention. The right column shows SentencePiece processing the identical string without any pre-tokenization step, passing raw bytes directly to Viterbi segmentation and marking word-initial pieces with '▁'. The comparison table at the bottom summarizes the key differences.
When to Use It

Use WordPiece when working with BERT-family models. These models were pretrained with WordPiece tokenizers and their embedding tables are indexed by WordPiece IDs. Switching to any other tokenizer requires retraining the embedding layer at minimum, and usually requires full pretraining. Use WordPiece for English-heavy tasks where the whitespace split convention is stable — classification, named entity recognition, question answering on English documents.

Use SentencePiece when working with LLaMA, T5, mT5, or XLNet. Also use SentencePiece when building multilingual systems: its raw-byte processing handles any script without code changes. The Unigram LM variant is preferable when subword regularization is desired during training to improve model robustness.
BERT, Electra, Albert: use WordPiece via BertTokenizer or AutoTokenizer — do not swap the tokenizer.
LLaMA 2/3, T5, mT5: use the bundled SentencePiece model — it ships inside the model repository and AutoTokenizer loads it automatically.
New multilingual model from scratch: train SentencePiece with Unigram LM on a balanced multilingual corpus with character_coverage=0.9995 to cover all Unicode scripts.
Deterministic production inference with SentencePiece: always set nbest_size=0 to disable sampling and get the Viterbi-optimal segmentation.
How to Implement

Both algorithms are available through well-maintained Python libraries. For WordPiece, HuggingFace 'transformers' and 'tokenizers' provide the complete implementation used in BERT. For SentencePiece, the 'sentencepiece' Python package provides training and inference in one install. Training a custom SentencePiece model is rarely necessary — for most deployments you load the tokenizer bundled with the pretrained model.
Training a Custom SentencePiece Tokenizer

# pip install sentencepiece
import sentencepiece as spm

# Train a SentencePiece Unigram model on a plain text corpus.
# The corpus file should have one sentence per line, UTF-8 encoded.
# No pre-tokenization needed — SentencePiece handles raw text.

spm.SentencePieceTrainer.train(
    input="corpus.txt",          # one sentence per line
    model_prefix="my_tokenizer", # output: my_tokenizer.model + my_tokenizer.vocab
    vocab_size=32000,
    character_coverage=0.9995,   # fraction of Unicode chars to cover
    model_type="unigram",        # or "bpe" for SentencePiece BPE variant
    pad_id=0,
    unk_id=1,
    bos_id=2,
    eos_id=3,
    pad_piece="[PAD]",
    unk_piece="[UNK]",
    bos_piece="<s>",
    eos_piece="</s>",
)

print("Training complete. Files: my_tokenizer.model, my_tokenizer.vocab")
Using a Trained SentencePiece Model

import sentencepiece as spm

# Load a trained model
sp = spm.SentencePieceProcessor(model_file="my_tokenizer.model")

text = "unhappiness is the opposite of joy"
ids = sp.encode(text, out_type=int)     # token IDs
pieces = sp.encode(text, out_type=str)  # token strings
print("IDs:   ", ids)
print("Pieces:", pieces)
# Example: ['\u2581un', 'hap', 'pi', 'ness', '\u2581is', '\u2581the', ...]

# Decode back to string (lossless — spaces restored via \u2581 replacement)
decoded = sp.decode(ids)
print("Decoded:", decoded)
assert decoded == text

# Subword regularization: sample alternative segmentation (training only)
sampled = sp.sample_encode_as_pieces(text, nbest_size=-1, alpha=0.1)
print("Sampled:", sampled)  # different segmentation each call

# Deterministic inference: use default encode() — returns best segmentation
best = sp.encode(text, out_type=str)
print("Best segmentation:", best)
Using WordPiece via HuggingFace

# pip install transformers
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Load BERT's WordPiece tokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

text = "unhappiness is the opposite of joy"
enc = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")

# Inspect the subword pieces
tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(enc["input_ids"][0])
print("Tokens:", tokens)
# Output: ['[CLS]', 'un', '##happiness', 'is', 'the', 'opposite', 'of', 'joy', '[SEP]']
# 'un' has no ## (word-initial), '##happiness' does (word-internal)

print("IDs:   ", enc["input_ids"][0].tolist())

# Decode back (the tokenizer handles ## concatenation automatically)
decoded = tokenizer.decode(enc["input_ids"][0], skip_special_tokens=True)
print("Decoded:", decoded)  # "unhappiness is the opposite of joy"

# Tokenizer metadata
print("Vocab size:", tokenizer.vocab_size)   # 30522 for bert-base-uncased
print("[UNK] id:  ", tokenizer.unk_token_id)
print("[SEP] id:  ", tokenizer.sep_token_id)
Examples
Example 1: Comparing WordPiece and SentencePiece on the Same Text

# pip install transformers sentencepiece
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Sample texts with different tokenization challenges
texts = [
    "unhappiness",                 # morphological split
    "Tokenization",                # capitalized
    "GPT-4",                       # hyphen + number
    "antidisestablishmentarianism", # long compound
]

# WordPiece via BERT (multilingual, case-sensitive)
bertok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-multilingual-cased")

# SentencePiece via T5 (always available without separate download)
t5tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("t5-small")

print(f"{'Text':<32}  {'WordPiece (mBERT)':<35}  SentencePiece (T5)")
print("-" * 95)
for text in texts:
    wp = bertok.convert_ids_to_tokens(bertok.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False))
    sp = t5tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(t5tok.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False))
    print(f"{text:<32}  {str(wp):<35}  {sp}")
Example 2: Training a WordPiece Tokenizer from Scratch

# pip install tokenizers
from tokenizers import Tokenizer
from tokenizers.models import WordPiece
from tokenizers.trainers import WordPieceTrainer
from tokenizers.pre_tokenizers import Whitespace
from tokenizers.normalizers import BertNormalizer

tokenizer = Tokenizer(WordPiece(unk_token="[UNK]"))
tokenizer.normalizer = BertNormalizer(lowercase=True)
tokenizer.pre_tokenizer = Whitespace()

trainer = WordPieceTrainer(
    vocab_size=10000,
    min_frequency=2,
    special_tokens=["[UNK]", "[CLS]", "[SEP]", "[PAD]", "[MASK]"],
    continuing_subword_prefix="##",   # the WordPiece convention
)

corpus = [
    "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog",
    "natural language processing with transformer models",
    "unhappiness is the opposite of happiness and joy",
    "tokenization splits words into subword pieces for efficiency",
] * 200  # repeat for min_frequency

tokenizer.train_from_iterator(corpus, trainer=trainer)

enc = tokenizer.encode("unhappiness and happiness")
print("Tokens:", enc.tokens)
print("IDs:   ", enc.ids)

tokenizer.save("/tmp/wordpiece.json")
Real-World Example

The LLaMA-2 tokenizer is a SentencePiece BPE model with vocabulary size 32,000 trained on roughly 2 TB of text from a balanced multilingual corpus. The '▁' prefix encoding means every token has clearly defined position semantics: a '▁' prefix indicates the piece begins a new word, while a piece without '▁' is interior. This allows LLaMA-2 to tokenize any language without language-detection logic.

LLaMA-3 upgraded to a vocabulary size of 128,000 — four times larger. The rationale: a larger vocabulary reduces token fertility (tokens per character), making prompts shorter for the same text. For English, LLaMA-3's 128K tokenizer produces roughly 25–30% fewer tokens than LLaMA-2's 32K tokenizer on typical prose, directly reducing inference cost and increasing effective context window. For code, the difference is even larger because identifiers and keywords are more likely to be whole tokens in the larger vocabulary.

BERT's WordPiece vocabulary of 30,522 tokens was trained on BookCorpus and English Wikipedia. Its multilingual variant mBERT uses a shared WordPiece vocabulary of 119,547 tokens covering 104 languages, with vocabulary allocated proportionally to each language's share of training data. This means high-resource languages receive more vocabulary slots, while low-resource languages tokenize with higher fertility — a known limitation that prompted Google to develop mT5 with a SentencePiece tokenizer to achieve more balanced multilingual coverage.
Common Mistakes

Mixing tokenizers between pretraining and fine-tuning: even a minor version upgrade of a SentencePiece or WordPiece model can reassign IDs for some tokens, making fine-tuned checkpoints produce garbled output when loaded with the updated tokenizer.
Calling sp.encode() without out_type: the default out_type in some SentencePiece versions returns strings, not integers, silently breaking downstream code that expects integer IDs.
Using BERT's tokenizer with LLaMA weights: the integer ID 1000 refers to completely different tokens in each vocabulary. This produces wrong embeddings with no runtime error.

Forgetting special tokens for BERT: BERT expects [CLS] at the start and [SEP] at the end of every input. Calling encode() without add_special_tokens=True omits them, causing the [CLS] embedding used for classification to be missing.
Assuming '##' pieces decode with a space: '##ing' must be concatenated directly to the preceding token with no separator. Joining token strings with spaces produces 'play ##ing' instead of 'playing'.

Ignoring SentencePiece subword regularization in production: if sampling was used during training and is not disabled at inference time, the tokenizer returns different token IDs for the same string on each call, making output non-reproducible.
Best Practices
Always use AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained() with the exact model name. This guarantees correct normalization rules, special tokens, and vocabulary version.
Pin the transformers and sentencepiece library versions in requirements.txt alongside the model. Tokenizer behavior can change across library versions even for the same pretrained model name.
Load the SentencePieceProcessor once at startup and reuse it for all requests. Initialization has non-trivial overhead; encoding individual strings is fast once loaded.

In multilingual systems, measure token fertility (tokens per character) per language on a representative sample before committing to a vocabulary size. High fertility in target languages directly increases inference cost.
Never build a WordPiece tokenizer from scratch for a BERT-family task unless genuinely pretraining a new model. Retraining the tokenizer without retraining the model produces embedding mismatches.
Performance and Scalability

SentencePiece encoding is implemented in C++ with Python bindings, achieving roughly 1–2 million tokens per second on a single CPU core. The HuggingFace tokenizers library provides a Rust-backed WordPiece implementation at comparable throughput. Both are several orders of magnitude faster than any Python-native tokenization implementation, and tokenization is never the inference bottleneck — the model forward pass dominates by multiple orders of magnitude.

Unigram LM training is more expensive than BPE training: the pruning step requires evaluating corpus likelihood for every candidate token at each iteration. For a 32,000-token vocabulary trained on 100 GB of text, Unigram LM training typically takes 2–4 hours on a single CPU, compared to 30–60 minutes for BPE on the same corpus. Neither is a practical bottleneck since tokenizer training is a one-time cost.
SentencePiece C++ library: 1–2M tokens/sec per core at inference; well under 1 ms per sentence for typical prompt lengths.
HuggingFace tokenizers (Rust): similar throughput to SentencePiece C++ for WordPiece; supports batch encoding with parallel threads via tokenizer.encode_batch().
Memory: a loaded SentencePiece model occupies 2–20 MB in RAM; a BERT-style WordPiece vocabulary file is typically 200–500 KB — negligible even in memory-constrained deployments.
Tooling and Ecosystem
sentencepiece (Google): the canonical C++ library with Python bindings for training and inference. Supports both BPE and Unigram LM. Install: pip install sentencepiece.

transformers (HuggingFace): AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained() automatically loads WordPiece for BERT-family models and SentencePiece for T5/LLaMA-family models. The unified interface hides the underlying algorithm. Install: pip install transformers.
tokenizers (HuggingFace): Rust-backed library for training both WordPiece and BPE from scratch. Used internally by transformers. Install: pip install tokenizers.

llama-cpp-python: bundles LLaMA's SentencePiece tokenizer alongside GGUF model weights for local CPU inference. Provides a Python API that automatically loads the matching tokenizer from the model file.

HuggingFace Tokenizer Viewer (online): an interactive tool that shows WordPiece and SentencePiece tokenizations for any input string and model checkpoint — useful for quickly understanding how different tokenizers split the same text.

Security and Reliability

Tokenizer mismatches are one of the most common silent failure modes in LLM deployment. The failure is silent because the model still runs — it processes a completely different sequence of token IDs than intended. Always load the tokenizer from the same source as the model weights, and pin both to the same revision hash or version tag.

Unicode normalization inconsistency: if one part of a pipeline normalizes text to NFC and another uses NFKC, the same surface form produces different bytes and thus different tokens. Enforce a single normalization at the pipeline entry point.

Prompt injection via special tokens: validate that no input string produces special token IDs ([SEP], [CLS], </s>) that were not deliberately inserted. An adversarial input can craft byte sequences that tokenize into structural separator tokens.

Context window overflow: always measure token count explicitly before dispatching a request. Non-ASCII text and code tokenize at significantly higher fertility than plain English prose — never estimate from character or word count.
Mental Model

Think of a tokenizer vocabulary as a compression dictionary for human text. BPE is a greedy encoder that builds this dictionary by always combining the two most common adjacent symbols — like a compression tool that only looks one step ahead. WordPiece is slightly smarter: instead of the most common pair, it combines the pair that most often appears together relative to how often the two pieces appear independently — it looks for genuine affinity, not just frequency. SentencePiece with Unigram LM builds the dictionary from the opposite direction: start with every possible short substring, then discard the ones whose removal hurts compression least — like an editing process that produces a maximally useful result rather than an incrementally accumulated one.

The '##' prefix in WordPiece and the '▁' prefix in SentencePiece both solve the same problem — how to reconstruct original word boundaries from a flat sequence of subword pieces — but from opposite directions. WordPiece marks pieces that are not at the start of a word (word-internal). SentencePiece marks pieces that are at the start of a word (word-initial). Both conventions are losslessly invertible; the difference only matters when integrating a tokenizer with a specific model whose embeddings were trained with that convention.
Key Takeaways

WordPiece selects merges by likelihood gain (PMI) rather than raw frequency, producing a more globally optimal vocabulary; it marks word-internal pieces with '##' and is the tokenizer for BERT and all its derivatives.

SentencePiece processes raw bytes without pre-tokenization, making it natively language-agnostic; its '▁' prefix marks word-initial pieces and enables lossless detokenization by replacing '▁' with a space.
SentencePiece Unigram LM builds vocabulary top-down by pruning a large candidate set rather than bottom-up by merging, and supports subword regularization for training-time data augmentation.
Always load the tokenizer bundled with the model checkpoint — tokenizer and embedding table are co-trained and must match exactly. Mismatches cause silent, hard-to-debug output errors.

Vocabulary size is the primary lever for trading sequence length against coverage: LLaMA-3's 128K vocabulary produces roughly 25% shorter sequences than LLaMA-2's 32K vocabulary on the same text, directly reducing inference cost.

SentencePiece achieves more balanced fertility across languages than WordPiece, which requires language-specific pre-tokenization rules and produces uneven token counts across scripts in multilingual settings.
Token count, not word count, determines context window usage and API cost — non-ASCII text, code, and JSON tokenize at significantly higher fertility than plain English prose.
